document.title: National Integrated Electricity Policy (NIEP-SIP)
document.source: Federal Ministry of Power, Federal Republic of Nigeria

knowledge_entries.policy_promised: Privatisation of generation and distribution sub-sectors (cornerstone of NEPP 2001 and EPSRA 2005) would attract private capital, introduce competition, improve efficiency, and transform the electricity sector.
knowledge_entries.consumer_experienced: After 20+ years, privatisation has not yielded its intended benefits. The government's own 2025 policy document states explicitly: 'the intended results of privatisation have not been achieved.'
data_points: 11 DisCos transferred to 60% private ownership between 2011–2013
1 additional DisCo (Aba) fully privatised
Only 1 notable IPP commissioned since the 2005 Act
FGN forced to intervene financially in the distribution sector since 2014
Transmission assets remain entirely under government ownership despite reform

knowledge_entries.policy_promised: Reforms would unlock Nigeria's generation capacity and deliver reliable electricity to meet growing residential, commercial, and industrial demand.
knowledge_entries.consumer_experienced: Nigeria has 13,000MW installed capacity but delivers only ~4,200MW on a typical day — 32% utilisation. Available capacity is 7,652MW but only 3,500–4,200MW reaches the grid daily.
data_points: Installed capacity: ~13,000MW
Available capacity (2023–2024): 7,652MW
Daily average grid-connected capacity: 3,500–4,200MW
This represents only ~24% of recorded peak demand
Gap caused by: gas supply constraints, maintenance failures, distribution sector non-creditworthiness, financial insolvency

knowledge_entries.policy_promised: Reformed, privatised distribution companies would improve service delivery and reliability to all customer classes.
knowledge_entries.consumer_experienced: Weak distribution systems deliver an average of 6–7 hours of uninterrupted supply daily to most customers. Band A customers (the best-served) receive 20+ hours — but they are a small minority.
data_points: Average daily supply: 6–7 hours for most consumers
Band A threshold (20+ hours/day) used to justify April 2024 subsidy removal
Distribution asset base described as 'obsolete and poorly maintained'
Frequent load shedding and outages are the norm
ATC&C (Aggregate Technical, Collection and Commercial) losses are 'unacceptably high'

knowledge_entries.policy_promised: NEPP 2001 included a rural electrification sub-policy aimed at rapidly expanding access. EPSRA 2005 established a Rural Electrification Fund. Universal access by 2030 is the current target.
knowledge_entries.consumer_experienced: An estimated 94 million Nigerians (45% of the population) have no electricity access at all, with the vast majority in rural communities. Nigeria holds the largest energy access deficit in sub-Saharan Africa.
data_points: 94 million Nigerians without electricity access (IEA data cited)
National electrification rate: ~55% (as of 2019)
Urban electrification rate: 84%
Rural electrification rate: 26%
Nigeria: most populous country in Africa, 4th largest economy — yet largest energy access deficit in sub-Saharan Africa
55% of population lacks electricity connections (used in universal access section)

knowledge_entries.policy_promised: EPSRA 2005 established the Power Consumer Assistance Fund (PCAF) to subsidise electricity for underprivileged consumers. Tariffs would be set to ensure affordable electricity for all Nigerians.
knowledge_entries.consumer_experienced: In April 2024, electricity subsidies were removed for Band A consumers (those receiving 20+ hours/day). Policymakers concluded subsidies are 'no longer sustainable'. Unclear PCAF eligibility criteria leave many without affordable electricity.
data_points: Subsidy removal effective: April 2024
Band A consumers: 20+ hours supply/day — the relatively better-served urban segment
Service-based tariff methodology implemented — customers reclassified by hours received
PCAF described as having 'unclear eligibility criteria' that 'hinder its effectiveness'
46% poverty rate (World Bank 2023) — ~104 million Nigerians may be unable to afford electricity
MSMEs spend 30–40% of income on electricity costs

knowledge_entries.policy_promised: A reliable grid would make self-generation unnecessary. Reforms would reduce dependence on expensive diesel generators.
knowledge_entries.consumer_experienced: Nigeria has built a 45GW parallel generator economy — more than 3x the grid's delivered capacity — entirely because the public grid failed. This imposes massive costs on households and businesses.
data_points: Private generator capacity: 45GW
Grid-connected capacity delivered: ~4GW
Nigeria has one of the highest genset usage rates globally
Generator emissions significantly outweigh emissions from grid-connected gas power plants
MSMEs spend 30–40% of income on electricity (mostly diesel/gas generators)
Manufacturing production output declined 9.7% in 2022 due to energy costs
161 million Nigerians lack clean cooking access — compounding fossil fuel dependency

knowledge_entries.policy_promised: Reforms including TCN investment, private sector participation, and independent system operation would create a stable, reliable national grid.
knowledge_entries.consumer_experienced: The national grid experienced 564 partial or complete collapses between 2000 and 2022 — roughly one every two weeks for 22 years. System operation still relies on manual systems with no SCADA/EMS.
data_points: 564 partial or complete grid collapses: 2000–2022
System Operator still uses manual systems — no automated real-time grid management
Shortage of ancillary services forces reliance on under-frequency relays for grid stability
Northwest Nigeria served by radial single-circuit lines — any outage blacks out entire region
Transmission technical losses: 7–9%
SCADA/EMS project incomplete as of 2025 — grid cannot integrate intermittent renewables without it

knowledge_entries.policy_promised: Reliable, affordable electricity would power economic growth, support SMEs, and create jobs across Nigeria.
knowledge_entries.consumer_experienced: MSMEs — which account for 96% of all businesses, 49% of GDP, and employ 84% of the workforce — spend 30–40% of income on electricity. Power failures caused a 9.7% decline in manufacturing production output in 2022.
data_points: MSMEs: 96% of all Nigerian businesses
MSMEs contribution to GDP: 49%
MSMEs share of national workforce: 84%
MSMEs electricity spend: 30–40% of income (Manufacturers Association of Nigeria, 2023)
Manufacturing production output decline: 9.7% in 2022
Cost passed to consumers via price increases — declining product demand cycle
Healthcare, hospitality, transport, communication sectors all cited as 'crippled by frequent power outages'

knowledge_entries.policy_promised: Rural electrification sub-policy (NEPP 2001) and Rural Electrification Fund (EPSRA 2005) would rapidly and cost-effectively expand electricity access in rural and semi-urban areas.
knowledge_entries.consumer_experienced: Rural electricity access stands at 26% — against 84% in urban areas. Rural communities rely on petrol/diesel generators or kerosene lamps. Women and girls bear a double burden of energy poverty and unremunerated labour.
data_points: Rural electrification rate: 26%
Urban electrification rate: 84%
54.28% of Nigerians live in urban areas; remainder rural
40% of primary health centres lack adequate power for standard procedures
Rural women: double burden of energy poverty + collecting fuelwood
REA programmes (NEP, DARES) underway but access gap remains severe
DARES project: $750M IDA credit targeting 17.5 million Nigerians — scale of unmet need

knowledge_entries.policy_promised: Universal energy access would reduce poverty, improve health outcomes, and support human development.
knowledge_entries.consumer_experienced: 94,300 Nigerians die prematurely each year from illnesses linked to indoor smoke from polluting stoves — the 3rd leading cause of mortality after malaria and HIV/AIDS. Energy poverty directly kills.
data_points: Premature deaths from indoor smoke annually: 94,300 (WHO estimate)
Ranking: 3rd cause of mortality in Nigeria behind malaria and HIV/AIDS
161 million Nigerians lack access to clean cooking fuels and technologies
Between 2000–2010: over 50% of Nigeria's primary forests destroyed — 70% of population using firewood
Poverty rate 2023: 46% (~104 million people) — World Bank
Hospitals serving the poor affected by erratic power supply — rising mortality rates cited
Energy poverty disproportionately affects women and marginalised groups

knowledge_entries.policy_promised: Market reforms and firm contracts would ensure reliable gas supply to power plants, securing generation capacity.
knowledge_entries.consumer_experienced: Gas supply contracts operate on 'best endeavour' — not firm obligations. Gas suppliers won't commit because DisCos won't pay, DisCos can't pay because consumers can't afford cost-reflective tariffs. A circular debt trap that has persisted for decades.
data_points: Gas supply contracts: 'best endeavour basis' — no firm supply obligations
Reason: counterparty payment risk from DisCos
Gas constraints include: inadequate volume, low delivery pressure, out-of-specification gas quality
Pipeline vandalism compounds supply unreliability
NESI described as financially insolvent
Debts across the electricity value chain — including to gas suppliers — collated and audited but unresolved
Gas-to-power contracts: Naira/dollar currency mismatch adds further instability

knowledge_entries.policy_promised: Smart metering rollout was a priority post-privatisation. Consumers would be billed accurately for electricity actually consumed.
knowledge_entries.consumer_experienced: Multiple smart meter rollout efforts have 'consistently failed to deliver effective results'. Consumers are billed on estimates. The metering gap remains a critical unresolved issue in 2025.
data_points: Described as: 'various efforts at rolling out smart meters that have consistently failed'
Metering gap review mandated as a critical early action in NIEP 2025
Shortages in prepaid electricity metering make it difficult for persons with disabilities to pay bills
Estimated billing (no meter) is a known source of consumer disputes and revenue leakage
Policy now calls for comprehensive metering gap review in collaboration with DisCos, end-users, manufacturers, and financial institutions

knowledge_entries.policy_promised: Investment in TCN and transmission infrastructure would create a resilient, redundant national grid.
knowledge_entries.consumer_experienced: Most of the Northwest and the entire Northeast are served by radial single-circuit transmission lines. A single fault blacks out entire regions. Despite years of investment, equipment obsolescence and 7–9% technical losses persist.
data_points: Northwest: radial single-circuit lines — any outage = regional blackout
Northeast: entirely on single-circuit lines
Transmission technical losses: 7–9% (described as 'relatively high')
Equipment obsolescence persists despite 'massive investment' post-privatisation
Investment primarily from FG-backed concessionary loans — not private capital
TCN still entirely government-owned
SCADA/EMS (automation system) incomplete — grid operated manually

knowledge_entries.policy_promised: The National Electric Power Policy 2001 required reviews every five years to address evolving challenges.
knowledge_entries.consumer_experienced: No formal policy review was initiated for 22 years — from 2001 until September 2023. The 2025 NIEP is the first update. Successive ministers were 'consumed with oversight of a sector always in crisis', allowing policymaking to lapse entirely.
data_points: NEPP 2001 required half-decade reviews — none conducted until 2023
22-year policy vacuum: 2001–2023
Reason cited: no compulsory mandate on Ministers for policymaking; crisis management consumed attention
Electricity Act 2023 now mandates review every 5 years with FEC approval
The 2025 NIEP is thus the first substantive electricity policy update in over two decades

meta.project: Vitics — Nigeria AI Energy Intelligence Platform
meta.purpose: Grounded knowledge base: policy promises vs consumer reality
